change_summary: Decider closed ISS-1899 (Raghuraman→Loeb citation fix) post-c3; queued WIN-066 back for verification of the remaining moderate hole (ISS-1906, TLDR/detail tension). c3 also surfaced two minor holes (ISS-1908 range-derivation, ISS-1909 sign-non-discriminating) and a defense-survives=3 hole (ISS-1917) — none of these were patched between c3 and c4 because they require analyst content rewrites, not decider self-applies. This c4 verifies which c3 findings persist and adds one new observation about tldr_verdict (which carries the same defect as tldr_evidence but was not explicitly named in ISS-1906) plus a tightening of the lower-bound-90 W/m² claim against my own re-integration.
our_argument_summary: Verdict 'Std Model Explains' remains correct. Hemispheric energy balance is near-symmetric (Stephens et al. 2012, doi:10.1038/ngeo1580); the 0.34 W/m²/decade NH-minus-SH absorbed-solar-radiation trend (Loeb et al. 2025, PNAS, doi:10.1073/pnas.2511595122 — citation fix from c3 hole #1 is now applied in detail_evidence) is a 24-year aerosol/albedo/cloud-driven trend, not a static asymmetry. The dome supplies no radiation-budget derivation for the +0.34 W/m² value; if any natural derivation is attempted from dome parameters (h=5,733 km, R=20,015 km, sun orbiting at radius rs), inverse-cube projected flux falloff produces an inner/outer (NH/SH-mapped) flux ratio of order 2–6 across the seasonal sun-orbit range, predicting an NH-SH difference of order 100–300 W/m² — hundreds to a thousand times larger than the observed trend. Three independent failures stack: post-hoc adoption of the value, wrong-magnitude geometric prediction, and inability of static dome geometry to produce a temporal trend.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Unchanged from c3: no dome-side calculation pipeline for WIN-066. No energy-budget script, no CERES pull, no hemispheric-comparison logic. The dome lists WIN-066 in the V51 win catalogue and adopts the published CERES-derived figure as confirmation of geometric asymmetry without supplying a derivation.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Not applicable — no validation logic of any kind exists for this WIN.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. monitoring=none and derives_from_dome=false remain correctly tagged.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: No dome monitor code references WIN-066, hemispheric energy, NH/SH absorbed solar radiation, or CERES (verified in c2 against fuller raw-text snapshot, re-confirmed against current snapshot which has no contradicting evidence; analyst globe-fingerprint code_search.found_in agrees: 'No direct reference to WIN-066 or hemispheric energy in dome raw text').
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: The hemispheric ASR asymmetry is quantified by CERES (NASA satellite suite) and analyzed in published climate science (Stephens et al. 2012 baseline, Loeb et al. 2025 trend). The dome contributes no new numerical prediction — it adopts the published value 0.34 W/m²/decade and reframes it as confirmation of geometric asymmetry, identical magnitude.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Loeb et al. 2025 was published online 2025-09-29 (print 2025-10-07, PNAS issue 40), well before V51.0 (2026). The CERES dataset is publicly indexed and has been since the 2000s. The dome's adoption of 0.34 W/m²/decade post-dates the publication.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No dome formula starts from disc_radius, firmament_height, sun_altitude, or any other dome parameter and arrives at a hemispheric flux value. The dome page asserts that geometric asymmetry 'predicts' an NH excess but provides no flux integral, no inverse-square or inverse-cube falloff calculation, and no aether-damping coefficient. As shown in quantitative_verification below, if the derivation were attempted from dome parameters, the result would be 100–300 W/m² for plausible dome sun-orbit radii — orders of magnitude larger than the observed 0.34 W/m²/decade trend.

holes_found.description: ISS-1906 PERSISTS — TLDR/DETAIL TENSION on dome geometric capability. tldr_evidence still ends 'The dome\'s geometry (concentrated energy near the center, less at the rim) can\'t produce a hemisphere-dependent asymmetry,' and tldr_verdict still says 'The dome\'s axially symmetric geometry can\'t produce hemisphere-dependent effects.' But detail_evidence explicitly computes that under the natural inner-half=NH / outer-half=SH mapping, the dome WOULD produce an NH-SH flux difference of 90–270 W/m², three orders of magnitude too large. A motivated dome defender reading the TLDR will see 'can\'t produce' and the detail's '2.3× ratio, 90–270 W/m²' as a self-contradiction, weaponizing our own evidence. This is the moderate hole the queue item explicitly asks me to verify; verifying: still open, no patch landed between c3 (2026-05-09T06:45Z) and c4 (now). Note that ISS-1906 as filed by the decider names only tldr_evidence, but tldr_verdict carries the same defect with the same severity — the patch must touch BOTH fields.
holes_found.recommendation: Two-field rewrite. Suggested tldr_evidence: 'Earth\'s hemispheric energy balance is remarkably symmetric (Stephens et al. 2012). The small +0.34 W/m²/decade NH excess is a recently-detected trend driven by aerosols, ice loss, and cloud shifts — not a static feature. If you take dome geometry seriously and map the disc\'s inner half to NH and outer half to SH, the dome would predict an NH-SH flux difference hundreds of times larger than observed.' Suggested tldr_verdict: 'The hemispheric energy balance is nearly symmetric, with the small trend explained by time-varying aerosol/albedo effects. Worse for the dome: its disc geometry, taken seriously, would predict an NH-SH asymmetry of order 100–300 W/m² — wrong by several orders of magnitude.' This brings the strongest argument (over-prediction) to the front instead of burying it in detail.

holes_found.description: ISS-1908 PERSISTS — RANGE-WIDTH UNDERIVED. detail_evidence still says '~2.3× the solar energy of the outer half' and 'on the order of 90–270 W/m²' without explaining where the range comes from. My own re-integration this cycle (numerical integration F = F0·h/(r²+h²)^1.5 azimuth-averaged over sun orbit at radius rs, h=5,733 km, R=20,015 km, equal-area inner/outer split at r=R/√2≈14,153 km, mean absorbed=240 W/m² normalization) gives: rs=4,000 → ratio 5.5, NH-SH 332 W/m²; rs=8,000 → ratio 3.7, NH-SH 275 W/m²; rs=10,000 → ratio 2.7, NH-SH 219 W/m²; rs=11,000 → ratio 2.2, NH-SH 181 W/m²; rs=14,000 → ratio 1.1, NH-SH 30 W/m²; rs=18,000 → ratio 0.5, NH-SH −149 W/m² (sign FLIPS — dome predicts SH > NH at this rs). The dome\'s seasonal sun-orbit radius is constrained by AE projection of the tropics: ~7,400 km at June solstice (Tropic of Cancer at 23.5°N maps to inner annulus) to ~12,600 km at December solstice (Tropic of Capricorn at 23.5°S). Across this realistic range NH-SH varies from ~290 to ~100 W/m², averaging roughly 180–200 W/m². So the lower bound 90 in our prose is barely defensible — it requires rs at the high end of the seasonal range — and the prose doesn\'t say so. (c3 quantitative_verification noted the same issue and recommended either deriving the range or using a single representative value; the recommendation has not been implemented.)
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) tighten the prose to '~150–300 W/m² across the dome\'s seasonal sun-orbit radius (Tropic of Cancer ≈7,400 km in June through Tropic of Capricorn ≈12,600 km in December), with annual mean of order 180–200 W/m²' and explicitly identify rs as the controlling parameter; or (b) collapse to a single representative value at the dome\'s stated solstice configuration and note off-solstice values are also wildly over the observed 0.34 W/m²/decade. Option (a) is more bulletproof and ties the magnitude failure to the dome\'s own geometric parameters. NEW SUB-FINDING this cycle: at rs ≳14,000 km the dome geometry actually predicts SH > NH (sign flip), which never occurs in practice for the dome\'s seasonal sun orbit but does sharpen the over-prediction story — 'the dome doesn\'t even predict the right sign at all sun positions, let alone the right magnitude.' Optional addition to detail_evidence; not blocking.

holes_found.description: ISS-1909 PERSISTS — DIRECTION-MATCH NON-DISCRIMINATION. detail_verdict_text still does not preempt the dome-defender claim 'at least we got the sign right.' The sign NH > SH is independently predicted by mainstream climate science from differential land-mass distribution (NH ~39% land vs SH ~19% land → lower NH albedo), so a dome that predicts only the sign — not the magnitude or the temporal trend — gets zero discriminating credit. Same loophole as in WIN-058/-059/-060 (NMP/SAA drift direction-matching). c3 already recommended a one-sentence preemption; not yet patched. Severity is minor because the magnitude failure already carries the WIN, but a single preemptive sentence would close the loophole cheaply.
holes_found.recommendation: Append one sentence to detail_verdict_text after the post-hoc paragraph: 'Note also that the SIGN of the asymmetry (NH > SH) is independently predicted by standard climate science from differential land-mass distribution and aerosol forcing, so getting the direction right is non-discriminating evidence: both models predict the same sign, only one predicts roughly the right magnitude.'

holes_found.description: ISS-1917 PERSISTS — DEFENSE PIVOT (defense_survives=3). The advocate-mode counter-argument from c3 has not been preempted in the prose: a dome defender can pivot to 'sign and direction match; magnitude critique strawmans the dome by assuming pure E-M physics applies; aetheric damping rescues us.' c3 surfaced this and recommended a paragraph in detail_evidence acknowledging that direction-match is non-discriminating AND that any aether-damping rescue is unfalsifiable post-hoc fitting (no formula on dome site that derives 0.34 from dome parameters before the data). Still not patched. Recommend the c3 fix as written; this hole is concentric with ISS-1909 and the same paragraph addresses both.
holes_found.recommendation: Add the c3 advocate_mode preemptive_recommendation paragraph to detail_evidence: '(1) The DIRECTION of asymmetry (NH > SH) is independently predicted by mainstream landmass/aerosol arguments, so it is non-discriminating; (2) Any rescue invoking ad-hoc aether-damping that adjusts the geometric flux gradient to fit the observed value is unfalsifiable post-hoc fitting — there is no aether-damping formula on the dome site that derives 0.34 W/m² from dome parameters before the data, so the rescue is exactly the kind of just-so adjustment Part 3.1b warns against.' This single paragraph closes ISS-1909 and ISS-1917 simultaneously.

holes_found.description: NEW THIS CYCLE — INVERSE-SQUARE vs INVERSE-CUBE WORDING. detail_evidence reads 'a flat disc with a local sun at 5,733 km altitude creates extreme inverse-square flux falloff. Integration over the disc shows the inner half ... receives roughly 2.3× the solar energy of the outer half.' The 'inverse-square' phrase is a minor imprecision — pure point-source flux falls as 1/r², but the relevant projected flux on a horizontal disc surface (which is what the area-weighted integration uses) falls as cos(θ)·1/r² = h/r·1/r² = h/r³, i.e., inverse-CUBE in the strict horizontal-projection sense. The c3 quantitative_verification computation correctly used the inverse-cube form (F = F0·h/(r²+h²)^1.5) and arrived at 2.3:1, consistent with the prose. The prose word 'inverse-square' is technically the photon-density law, not the projected-flux law actually used in the integration. A picky physicist reader will catch this. Not load-bearing — the numerical conclusion is correct — but the prose word is wrong by a factor of cos(θ). Severity minor; one-word fix.
holes_found.recommendation: Change 'extreme inverse-square flux falloff' to 'extreme inverse-cube projected-flux falloff' (or 'extreme cos(θ)/r² projected-flux falloff'). Either reads correctly without changing the integration result.

stronger_arguments.description: The Loeb 2025 abstract attributes the emerging NH-SH ASR asymmetry to 'changes in hemispheric differences in aerosol-radiation interactions, surface albedo, and water vapor changes' over a 24-year baseline — i.e., entirely time-varying anthropogenic and ice-loss effects. A static dome geometry has zero way to produce a temporal trend. The dome's framing on the public site (the version captured in our raw-text/02b file does NOT include WIN-066, but the dome page lists this WIN as confirming 'persistent' geometric asymmetry per analyst globe-fingerprint notes) is therefore doubly wrong: the value isn't persistent, AND a static asymmetric geometry can't produce a 24-year trend even if it produced the right magnitude.
sources: Loeb et al. 2025 PNAS abstract (doi:10.1073/pnas.2511595122): 'changes in hemispheric differences in aerosol-radiation interactions, surface albedo, and water vapor changes... break in hemispheric symmetry... is linked with the atmosphere–ocean general circulation.'
data/wins.json:066.detail_evidence currently mentions 'aerosol changes, Arctic ice loss, and cloud shifts' but doesn't connect these to the static-vs-trend critique.

stronger_arguments.description: Cross-WIN consistency leverage (carried over from c3 — analyst should consider for an integration note). WIN-067 (Antarctic gravity hole) is verdicted Self-Contradicted because the dome's H(r) under a monotonic g↔H coupling forces a ≥38% gravity drop at the rim. WIN-066 makes the same shape of argument (center-rim flux gradient on a flat disc with a local source) but stays Std Model Explains because the dome supplies its own H(r) for gravity but no analogous radiation-budget formula. The verdict difference is defensible under our taxonomy (Self-Contradicted requires the dome's own equations to do the contradicting), but a future verdict-taxonomy widening — 'Self-Contradicted = the dome must predict a value contradictory to observation given any reasonable derivation from its stated geometry' — would promote WIN-066 to Self-Contradicted. Out of scope for this c4; flagged as a candidate for the next holistic-verdict-taxonomy review.
sources: data/wins.json:067 (current verdict Self-Contradicted, finding cites ≥38% rim gravity drop)
data/wins.json:066 (current verdict Std Model Explains, detail_evidence cites 90–270 W/m² over-prediction)

kernel_of_truth.description: The TLDR's 'axially symmetric geometry can\'t produce hemisphere-dependent effects' framing is technically defensible at the strict azimuthal level — the dome IS axially symmetric around the polar axis (data/wins.json:066.tldr_verdict; raw-text/04-coordinates.txt confirms NH and SH city catalogues are organized by latitude, with the NP at the disc center), and pure azimuthal symmetry alone cannot break a globe-style equator-bilateral hemispheric symmetry. The TLDR is also correct that the actual observation is small and its dominant explanation is land/ocean distribution + aerosols. So the TLDR is not LYING; it is selecting a true-but-weaker frame instead of the stronger over-prediction frame. The dome's intuition that an asymmetric Earth-system geometry should produce some hemispheric asymmetry is not crazy — the dome geometry is genuinely radially asymmetric, so a center-rim flux gradient really exists.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The TLDR's 'axially symmetric → can\'t produce hemispheric effects' frame is true only if you read 'hemispheric' as 'azimuthal,' which is the GLOBE'S geometry. On the dome's own geometry (data/wins.json:066.detail_evidence), NH and SH map to inner and outer radial annuli — and the dome's axial symmetry does NOT prevent center-rim radial gradients; it actively produces them. The strongest argument in the detail (over-prediction by 250–800×, computed from dome parameters by inverse-cube projected flux falloff) is therefore the rebuttal even if you grant the dome its own NH/SH mapping. Three failures stack independently: (1) post-hoc adoption (post_hoc=true tag in code_analysis), (2) wrong-magnitude when the geometric prediction is taken seriously (this review's quantitative_verification, 100–300 W/m² across realistic dome sun-orbit range), and (3) static dome geometry cannot produce a 24-year temporal trend (Loeb et al. 2025 abstract documents the trend is driven by time-varying aerosol/albedo/water-vapor changes). The TLDR\'s frame survives critique #2 only by silently switching from globe-style 'hemispheric' to dome-style 'radial-annular' — and once that switch is made, the dome is back in the over-prediction trap.

deeper_analysis: c4 confirms the verdict (Std Model Explains) and the principal argument structure remain sound. The c3 critical hole (Raghuraman→Loeb) was patched (ISS-1899 closed); detail_evidence now reads 'Loeb et al., 2025, PNAS' correctly. The four c3 non-critical holes are all still open (ISS-1906 moderate, ISS-1908 minor, ISS-1909 minor, ISS-1917 moderate) because they require analyst content rewrites rather than decider self-applies. ISS-1906 (the queue item's named focus) is the most user-visible because TLDRs render before the expansion clicks. New this cycle: (a) tldr_verdict carries the same defect as tldr_evidence and must be patched in lock-step (ISS-1906 as filed names only tldr_evidence — the analyst patch should cover both fields); (b) my own re-integration tightens c3\'s rs↔ratio mapping and shows the realistic dome seasonal sun-orbit range (rs ≈ 7,400–12,600 km from AE projection of tropics) gives NH-SH ≈ 100–300 W/m² rather than the 90–270 in our prose — the lower bound 90 inherits c2\'s framing; (c) the 'inverse-square' wording in detail_evidence is a minor physics imprecision (the integration uses inverse-cube projected flux). None of (a)/(b)/(c) changes the verdict or the headline argument. Recommended_action = minor_edit (consistent with c3). Decider should treat ISS-1906 as the priority patch and bundle the four other open holes into the same analyst rewrite to amortize cost.

citations_verified: Stephens et al. (2012, Nature Geoscience) doi:10.1038/ngeo1580 — verified in c3, no change needed.

Loeb et al. (2025, PNAS) doi:10.1073/pnas.2511595122 — verified in c3 via CrossRef and OpenAlex; current detail_evidence in wins.json now cites 'Loeb et al., 2025' correctly (post-c3 patch landed via ISS-1899 closure).

advocate_mode.best_defense: The strongest TLDR-level dome defense is: 'You\'re demanding the TLDR carry the over-prediction punchline, but the TLDR\'s job is to summarize the verdict, not the strongest counter-argument. The verdict is \'the hemispheric balance is symmetric and the small trend has standard-physics drivers\' — that\'s exactly what the TLDR says. The detail is for readers who want the second-order \'and the dome would be wrong by 250×\' argument. Stop demanding the TLDR replicate the detail.' This defense has merit (TLDRs do summarize verdicts, not arguments) but fails on a specific point: the TLDR\'s last sentence ('can\'t produce a hemisphere-dependent asymmetry') is itself an ARGUMENT, not just a verdict summary, and the argument it makes is weaker and arguably wrong. So the defense reduces to 'remove the bad argument from the TLDR' — which is what we want anyway. A second defense: 'The dome doesn\'t commit to inverse-cube flux falloff; you\'re imposing standard physics on a model that explicitly invokes aetheric corrections.' That defense fails because the dome supplies no alternative formula AND would have to predict the right magnitude (0.34 W/m²/decade, including the temporal trend) before the data, which it didn\'t.
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Same as ISS-1917 / c3: add a single paragraph to detail_evidence acknowledging direction-match is non-discriminating AND any aether-damping rescue is unfalsifiable post-hoc fitting. With ISS-1906 (TLDR rewrite to bring over-prediction to front) AND this paragraph in place, defense_survives drops from 3 to 1: the only surviving dome defense becomes 'we don\'t commit to ANY specific predicted value,' which is itself an admission the WIN isn\'t a prediction at all.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: Same WIN-066/WIN-067 verdict-asymmetry as c3 noted (Std Model Explains vs Self-Contradicted, both turning on disc-geometry center-rim arguments, distinguished by whether the dome supplies its own derivation). No new inconsistencies surfaced this cycle. Confirmed compatible: code_analysis tag pattern (monitoring=none, post_hoc=true, derives_from_dome=false) matches the broader 'standard-data-relabeled' WIN cluster (WIN-052, -054, -055, etc.), so the verdict and tags are internally consistent with how we treat the rest of that cluster.

claims_checked: Inner-half / outer-half flux ratio on dome geometry under realistic sun-orbit radii.
NH-SH absolute flux difference range (compared against the 90–270 W/m² prose).
Magnitude over-prediction factor against 0.34 W/m²/decade observed.
Verification that the citation patch (Raghuraman→Loeb) landed correctly in current wins.json.
quantitative_verification.computations: Numerical integration this cycle (Python; see review-attached scratch in tracker if needed): F(r) = F0·h/((r-rs·cosφ)²+(rs·sinφ)²+h²)^1.5 averaged over sun azimuth φ ∈ [0,2π), with h=5,733 km, R=20,015 km, equal-area inner/outer split at r=R/√2≈14,153 km, mean absorbed=240 W/m² normalization. Results across rs (km): 4,000 → ratio 5.5, NH 406, SH 74, NH-SH 332; 8,000 → ratio 3.7, NH 378, SH 102, NH-SH 275; 10,000 → ratio 2.7, NH 349, SH 131, NH-SH 219; 11,000 → ratio 2.2, NH 330, SH 150, NH-SH 181; 14,000 → ratio 1.1, NH 255, SH 225, NH-SH 30; 18,000 → ratio 0.5, NH 165, SH 315, NH-SH −149 (sign flips). Realistic dome seasonal sun-orbit range: rs ≈ 7,400 km (Tropic of Cancer at 23.5°N under AE projection) to rs ≈ 12,600 km (Tropic of Capricorn at 23.5°S). Across this range NH-SH varies from ~290 to ~100 W/m², annual mean ≈ 180–200 W/m². Over-prediction factor: 100 W/m² / 0.34 W/m² ≈ 290×; 290 W/m² / 0.34 W/m² ≈ 850×. So '250–800×' in our prose is correct. Citation patch: detail_evidence in current wins.json now reads 'Loeb et al., 2025, PNAS' (verified by inspection); 'Raghuraman' string no longer present. ISS-1899 closure successful.
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: Lower bound 90 W/m² in our prose is barely defensible against my realistic-rs range (annual minimum ≈100 W/m² at December solstice). The 90 anchor is inherited from c2's '93 W/m² NH-excess-above-mean' framing under a different normalization convention. Recommend tightening to ~100–300 W/m² (across the dome's realistic seasonal sun-orbit range) per ISS-1908. New observation: at rs > ~13,000 km the dome geometry actually flips sign (SH > NH); this is OUTSIDE the realistic seasonal range so it doesn't refute the dome on present configuration, but it sharpens the 'dome geometry is fragile' framing if we want it.
